texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is an emerging thermal-based membrane technology that has the capacity to treat a wide range of feed waters, including high salinity brines and wastewater. Compared with pressure-driven processes, MD has the following advantages: low electrical energy consumption, complete removal of non-volatile solutes  and  ambient  operating  pressures.  On  the  other  hand,  membrane  fouling  and  pore  wetting  remain  a challenge holding back its wider industrial implementation. To manage this issue, effective pretreatment and cleaning strategies are essential, as with other membrane processes. Despite the importance of these aspects for achieving a stable and reliable water treatment process, far too little attention has been paid to them in the MD literature. Evidence of this is that a comprehensive review on both membrane cleaning and pretreatment in MD is still lacking, despite the large and growing number of publications related to the technology. In this work, we first provide an overview of the three main types of fouling present in MD: inorganic, organic and biological fouling. After this we review, in detail, the use of pretreatment protocols in a range of MD applications, highlighting their efficacy in preventing fouling and ensuring stable performance. Next, we review the use of state-ofthe-art chemical and physical cleaning strategies and discuss the development of 'self-cleaning ' membranes that are superhydrophobic or omniphobic. Finally, future research directions are suggested, with the aim of helping researchers  working  in  this  field  to  develop  membrane  distillation  technology  towards  wider  commercial implementation.

texts.orig: During successful operation of MD, very few contaminants are able to pass through the pores along with the vapour, resulting in very high separation  efficiency  ( > 99.9%)  of  non-volatiles.  The  separated  contaminants can include salts, heavy metals, dyes and many others. Thus, membrane distillation has been demonstrated on a range of feed water types including seawater [2], mining effluent [3], textile wastewater [4], produced water [5] and more [6,7].

The  lower sensitivity  of  vapour  pressure  to  increases  in  the  feed  concentration compared to osmotic pressure enables MD to operate at higher salinities in  contrast  to  pressure-driven  processes  like  reverse  osmosis  (RO). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the above advantages and the intensive research into the MD process, one fundamental aspect of operating an MD plant which has received comparatively little attention is membrane cleaning and pretreatment. As with all membrane-based separation processes, ensuring the membrane surface is free from cake fouling layers and pore-blocking substances is essential for stable performance. In reality, pretreatment and cleaning protocols are required to manage the degree to which the fouling layer builds up over time. In pressure-driven membrane processes, the build-up of foulant molecules can block pores and thereby increase  the  resistance  to  permeate  flow.  However,  in  membrane distillation the fouling layer is typically ~ 90% hydrated [12], thus the evaporating water molecules are not necessarily physically impeded by the fouling layer. Instead, the fouling layer can reduce the temperature at the liquid -vapour interface and thereby reduce the driving force for vapour permeation [13].
texts.text: Despite the above advantages and the intensive research into the MD process, one fundamental aspect of operating an MD plant which has received comparatively little attention is membrane cleaning and pretreatment. As with all membrane-based separation processes, ensuring the membrane surface is free from cake fouling layers and pore-blocking substances is essential for stable performance. In reality, pretreatment and cleaning protocols are required to manage the degree to which the fouling layer builds up over time. In pressure-driven membrane processes, the build-up of foulant molecules can block pores and thereby increase  the  resistance  to  permeate  flow.  However,  in  membrane distillation the fouling layer is typically ~ 90% hydrated [12], thus the evaporating water molecules are not necessarily physically impeded by the fouling layer. Instead, the fouling layer can reduce the temperature at the liquid -vapour interface and thereby reduce the driving force for vapour permeation [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another key difference between MD and pressure-driven processes is that fouling in the former can have a more dramatic effect on rejection performance, due to pore wetting. The wetting phenomena occurs when the  liquid  entry  pressure  (LEP)  of  the  membrane  is  lower  than  the pressure on the feed side due to the accumulation of foulant particles on and in the membrane [14,15]. The degree of wetting in MD may be described as one of a) surface wetting (where liquid intrusion is withheld at the surfaces and not completely through the membrane, maintaining a vapour gap); b) partial wetting (where liquid has intruded through some  of  the  pores);  and  c)  full  wetting  (where  liquid  has  intruded through most or all of the pores can freely flow through to them) [16]. To learn more about the phenomenon of wettability, we refer the reader to a recent review article by Yao et al [17].

The wetting phenomena occurs when the  liquid  entry  pressure  (LEP)  of  the  membrane  is  lower  than  the pressure on the feed side due to the accumulation of foulant particles on and in the membrane [14,15]. The degree of wetting in MD may be described as one of a) surface wetting (where liquid intrusion is withheld at the surfaces and not completely through the membrane, maintaining a vapour gap); b) partial wetting (where liquid has intruded through some  of  the  pores);  and  c)  full  wetting  (where  liquid  has  intruded through most or all of the pores can freely flow through to them) [16]. To learn more about the phenomenon of wettability, we refer the reader to a recent review article by Yao et al [17].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In pressure-driven processes, blockage of the pores can reduce flux dramatically but may not affect rejection negatively (it may even increase rejection). In MD, if a hydrophilic bridge is created between the surface and the pore-interior, then the feed water can flow through as a liquid. In turn, this contaminates the permeate tank and deteriorates permeate  quality  and  is  of  particular  concern  during  long-term  MD operation [18].

In MD, if a hydrophilic bridge is created between the surface and the pore-interior, then the feed water can flow through as a liquid. In turn, this contaminates the permeate tank and deteriorates permeate  quality  and  is  of  particular  concern  during  long-term  MD operation [18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In 2015, Tijing et al. [19] published a thorough analysis of the approaches  to  controlling  fouling  in  MD  and  is  recommended  reading along  with  work  by  Warsinger  et  al.  [14,15].  However,  while  many studies have applied different cleaning and/or pretreatment regimes to demonstrate  their  efficacy  in  membrane  distillation  applications,  we believe it will be of value to the research community to review the most significant  of  these  and  highlight  recent  advances.  In  this  work,  we discuss the variety of pretreatment technologies available for MD and suggest different strategies depending on the feed solution and application. Then, we review the various cleaning strategies identified in the literature  which  fall  under  the  two  main  categories:  physical  and chemical and briefly discuss the development of superhydrophobic and omniphobic membranes that exhibit 'self-cleaning ' properties inspired by those of the lotus leaf. The purpose of this review is to provide researchers in academia and industry with a guide to pretreatment and membrane cleaning approaches that can facilitate the commercialisation of membrane distillation in a wide variety of applications.

texts.orig: Similarly, biofouling contributes significantly to water flux decline and pore wetting within membrane distillation and other membrane processes. This is particularly true in wastewater treatment applications where the feed water contains high quantities of bacteria, fungi and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) [27]. It has been shown that EPS acts as a pore-wetting agent even over short operating times [27]. The secretion of EPS forms a biofilm, and also attracts other microorganisms through quorum sensing [28]. texts.orig: Another  type  of  calcium  scaling  is  calcium  sulfate,  which,  after reaching a significant degree of supersaturation (e.g. 4 -7 g L -1 ), begins to rapidly crystallise not only on the membrane surface, but also in the membrane pores, causing their deformation and cracking (Fig. 7). Thus, sulfate precipitation may cause more severe and rapid wetting in MD [18]. For the above reasons, scaling can quickly damage the membranes in MD modules. In the work published by Warsinger et al. [14], membrane  damage  caused  by  CaCO3  and  CaSO4  scales  is  discussed.  The formation of cracks in the membranes and corresponding reductions in their  mechanical  strength  are  of  concern,  particularly  for  long-term operation, in addition to more conventional issues like pore blocking.

texts.orig: Silica scaling can also occur as most natural water resources contain small amounts of silica, which has low solubility. When concentrated beyond its solubility limit of approximately 120 mg/L [36], precipitation  can  form  a  hard  scale  that  is  extremely  difficult  to  remove.  In addition, silica scaling on heat exchanger surfaces can increase thermal resistance  and  consequently  elevates  the  cost  of  both  chemical  and mechanical cleaning procedures. Silica polymerization and the formation of amorphous colloidal silica are the main mechanisms for scale formation [37]. In the presence of low silica concentrations and neutral or acidic pH medium, soluble silica exists mostly in non-ionic form as monosilicic acid, Si(OH)4, while the monomeric form undergoes polymerization through a dehydration reaction to form polysilicic acids as concentration increases via silica-oxygen bonds (see Fig. 8) [37].

texts.orig: To reduce the effects of scaling, a number of strategies have been adopted  such  as  thermal  water  softening  [24],  pretreatment  with membrane separation  [24,39],  the  use  of  antiscalants  [40],  washing modules with dilute acids [41], modifying the pH of the feed and other process  conditions  [42],  magnetic  water  treatment  [43,44]  and  the development of advanced MD membranes [45,46]. These control strategies are discussed in the following section.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Pretreatments
texts.text: 3. Pretreatments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of MD can be greatly enhanced by the use of suitable pretreatment. As the operation of MD requires the surface tension of the feed water to be sufficient to hinder liquid permeation, it is particularly prone to wetting when the feed water contains low surface tension substances such as oils, surfactants, humic substances and combinations thereof.  While  one  response  to  this  issue  has  been  to  develop  selfcleaning  or  omniphobic  membranes,  another  approach  is  the  use  of pretreatment, of which there are many kinds. A selection of MD pretreatment strategies that the following section will discuss are depicted in  Fig.  9.  In  this  figure,  a  comparison  is  made  to  a  representative seawater  desalination  process  based  on  reverse  osmosis  technology [47], highlighting the relative simplicity of MD for this application.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Adsorption
texts.text: 3.1. Adsorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adsorption is a widely used pretreatment for membrane processes and uses high surface area materials such as activated carbon, graphene, metal organic frameworks, zeolites and others. Pollutants in water such as heavy metals, dyes or organic matter can adsorb to the surfaces of these materials and thereby be removed from water. It is considered a cost-effective  treatment  strategy  and  there  is  currently  very  active research  in  the  development  of  higher  performing  materials  for  the process [61]. Nevertheless, its use as a pretreatment step in MD has been limited but has been shown to be effective. Zhang et al. [49] used a combination of precipitative softening with Al2(SO4)3 ⋅ 18H2O (15 ppm, pH ~ 10) and adsorption/filtration with walnut shells to treat shale oil and  gas  produced  water  with  membrane  distillation.  They  chose  an aluminium-based salt over a ferric salt to prevent silica scaling (which is facilitated by iron). The turbidity of the feed was reduced by 94% and the filtration through walnut shells then removed > 95% of the toxic contaminants  in  the  water  such  as  BTEX  (benzene,  toluene,  ethylbenzene and xylenes). What is more, the MD ' s permeate quality was well within regulatory standards and the achieved water recovery was 80% with a final brine TDS of 180,000 mg/l.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a separate work, Zhang et al. [48] coupled adsorption by activated carbon  or  Amberlite  IRA67  with  VMD  to  concentrate  and  detoxify lignocellulosic hydrolyzates simultaneously for biofuel applications. In this case the adsorption process was used to increase the removal rates of acetic  acid  due  to  its  inhibitory  effects  on  fermentation,  rather  than improve the performance stability of membrane distillation. Nevertheless, the combined process was highly effective and achieved a 10-fold increase in ethanol production compared with the original fermentation process without treatment. This shows the wider potential of pretreatment  to  enable  targeted  resource  recovery  as  well  as  water recovery.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Coagulation-flocculation
texts.text: 3.2. Coagulation-flocculation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Coagulation-flocculation is a cost-effective pretreatment process for the removal of suspended solids and colloids from water. It is widely used to improve the efficiency of pressure-driven processes for wastewater  treatment  applications  and  it  has  proven  to  be  effective  in reducing fouling in membrane distillation as well. Li et al. [52] used poly-aluminium chloride (PACl) as a coagulant with a polyacrylamide (PAM) coagulant aid for biologically-treated coking wastewater prior to MD. Optimum PACl and PAM concentrations were 175 and 2 mg/L, respectively. While the MD system was capable of treating the raw water effectively, the use of PACl increased the rejection and flux during the experiments. Scaling by CaCO3 was also eliminated when the feed was groundwater which was pretreated using a contact clarifier (or accelator) [55]. Raw water was purified in the contact clarifier using coagulation with FeSO4 ⋅ 7H2O and softening with a Ca(OH)2  solution. Such action  did  not  allow  the  complete  removal  of  scalants  from  water, however, and so silica, magnesium, calcium, iron and titanium deposits were present on the membrane surface (Fig. 10). Hence, at high a water recovery ratio after several hundred hours of MD operation, the flux significantly decreased due to membrane fouling, made worse by the presence  of  the  chemicals.  Despite  this,  the  module  efficiency  was recovered using periodic washing with a 5% HCl solution but re-wetting occurred more rapidly in subsequent runs. [50] compared an FMX vortex filtration process (FMX-B) with flocculation-sedimentation (FS) and flocculationsedimentation-microfiltration  (FSMF)  processes  to  pretreat  real  shale gas wastewater (TDS > 120,000 mg/L). texts.orig: 3.3. Foam fractionation
texts.text: 3.3. Foam fractionation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Foam fractionation seeks to remove surface-active agents (surfactants) from the feed stream by injecting it with compressed air. This method  was  demonstrated  to  be  highly  effective  when  treating  real textile wastewater in a long term pilot study [53]. Immediate wetting was observed for the untreated feed but no wetting was observed when using  the  pretreated  feed  over  the  65-day  trial.  While  periodic  flux decline from ~ 5 L m -2 h 1 (LMH) to ~ 2 LMH was observed, it was noted that this could be restored to ~ 4 LMH with caustic cleaning of the membrane. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Advanced oxidation processes
texts.text: 3.4. Advanced oxidation processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Applications of AOPs in membrane distillation are also limited but notable.  Ricceri  et  al.  [51]  used  a  Fenton  oxidation  process  as  pretreatment for produced water desalination. Prior to using pretreatment, they observed no flux decline or permeate conductivity increase when the water contained paraffins and VOCs but they did notice an increase in total organic carbon (TOC) in the permeate tank. However, when the surfactant sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was present, they observed a dramatic increase in the permeate conductivity due to wetting of the PTFE  membrane ' s  pores,  as  reported  elsewhere  [4].  However,  when adding FeSO4 (19 mM) and H2O2, (32 mM) to the feed water, which had been acidified to pH 3, significant improvements in the water recovery were achieved, although less so when SDS was present. In the presence of  SDS,  the  maximum  recovery  was  40%  before  significant  wetting occurred -a marked improvement on the tests without pretreatment, which exhibited wetting almost immediately. In the absence of SDS the recovery  was  60 -70%,  as  limited  by  scaling  on  the  membrane. Furthermore,  in  the  latter  case,  the  TOC  levels  in  permeate  were considerably lower than when no pretreatment was used, suggesting effective breakdown of the organic substances was achieved. The authors note that if surfactants can be removed prior to MD, either by filtration  or  more  extensive  oxidation,  the  combination  of  Fenton oxidation and MD is effective for the treatment of produced water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Shin et al. [63] combined electrochemical oxidation with membrane distillation not only as a means to break down organic components in the water but also to generate the thermal driving force for the process by Ohmic heating. They used a boron-doped diamond (BDD) anode and a Ti cathode with potentials in the range of 1.25 -2.3 V. This generated · OH, SO3 · -and persulfate ions to breakdown a wide range of organic components in the feed effectively. They obtained steady fluxes and low permeate conductivity even with SDS present in the water (up to 0.4 mM), suggesting the greater efficacy of this process compared to the Fenton oxidation described above.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A photocatalytic approach to breaking down azo dyes was taken by Yatmaz et al. [54] using ZnO and TiO2  catalysts and UVA or UVC radiation prior to membrane distillation. While the purpose of the study was to improve COD removal in MD rather than to prevent fouling, it did show how effective photocatalytic oxidation processes can be enhanced by using MD to separate the catalysts as a post-treatment. Clearly, AOPs hold promise for applications in membrane distillation to help reduce fouling, increase rejection and enable the treatment of more complex water types using a simple and compact system. This is recommended as an area for further exploration.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Pre-filtration
texts.text: 3.5. Pre-filtration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pre-filtration with cartridge filters or micro/ultra/nanofiltration is widely used for a host of water treatment processes. It offers the ability not only to increase the overall stability of the treatment process but also to enable the fractionation of specific components from the feed water according to their size [64]. Quist-Jensen et al. [56] used a combination of UF and DCMD for the concentration of clarified orange juice. The UF step removed suspended solids and reduced the juice turbidity while the DCMD step was used to remove water and therefore concentrate the juice. The flux was fairly stable over 40 h of testing, with the initial and final values of 0.6 and 0.4 LMH, respectively. The relatively low fluxes were  due  to  the  low  temperature  operation  (24 ± 1 ◦ C)  which  is favourable for preserving flavour in the juice and it offers an advantage over conventional distillation processes.

texts.orig: One pilot study successfully demonstrated desalination of seawater onboard a ship using a simple candle filter as the only pretreatment step prior to vacuum membrane distillation. The thermal energy was provided by waste heat from the vessel engine and the system achieved a stable flux over 5 months and maintained a salt rejection of 99.99% [60]. This highlights the simplicity of membrane distillation compared to  RO,  which  requires  several  pretreatment  steps  when  desalinating seawater [47].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. The use of scale inhibitors
texts.text: 3.6. The use of scale inhibitors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Scale inhibitors (or antiscalants) are highly effective in controlling scaling  in  membrane  processes  and  are  widely  used  in  various  industries. They work by interacting with dissolved ions in solution and by adsorbing onto the surfaces of crystals or nuclei, inhibiting their further growth  and  subsequent  precipitation  out  of  solution.  This  is  a  timedependent process however, and may not eliminate the possibility of crystal formation altogether but rather increase the induction time long enough to enable membrane separation to take place [66]. They are widely  used  to  prevent  scaling  in  RO  processes  (being  more  costeffective than adding acids) and are therefore retained in the rejected RO brine. In many cases, the presence of antiscalants is beneficial to MD but it should be noted that in certain cases (such as when polyacrylic acid-based antiscalants are in the presence of iron), the formation of complexes with the antiscalant may result in increased organic fouling [67].
texts.text: Scale inhibitors (or antiscalants) are highly effective in controlling scaling  in  membrane  processes  and  are  widely  used  in  various  industries. They work by interacting with dissolved ions in solution and by adsorbing onto the surfaces of crystals or nuclei, inhibiting their further growth  and  subsequent  precipitation  out  of  solution.  This  is  a  timedependent process however, and may not eliminate the possibility of crystal formation altogether but rather increase the induction time long enough to enable membrane separation to take place [66]. They are widely  used  to  prevent  scaling  in  RO  processes  (being  more  costeffective than adding acids) and are therefore retained in the rejected RO brine. In many cases, the presence of antiscalants is beneficial to MD but it should be noted that in certain cases (such as when polyacrylic acid-based antiscalants are in the presence of iron), the formation of complexes with the antiscalant may result in increased organic fouling [67].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Martinetti et al. [58] used two commercial scale inhibitors (Preatreat Plus 0400 and Preatreat Plus Y2K, King Lee Technologies, San Diego, CA) to prevent scaling in MD and FO processes for the concentration of brackish water desalination brine. Without the scale inhibitors, the flux was stable up to a concentration factor of ~ 2 after which, rapid and continuous flux decline was observed. Surprisingly, when the scale inhibitor was present (at concentrations ranging from 1 to 8 ppm), the flux declines were more rapid and occurred sooner. This was attributed to the formation of silica gels on the membrane. However, after this, the flux recovered significantly on its own and when using a scale inhibitor concentration of 4 ppm the flux remained fairly stable up to a concentration factor of ~ 4, above which the flux again began to decline. The authors suggested that the hardening of the silica gels over time enabled their removal from the membrane surface and subsequent flux recovery. Cleaning the membranes (PTFE and PP) with a solution consisting of 0.029  M  disodium  ethylenediaminetetraacetic  acid  (Na2EDTA)  and 0.058  M  sodium  hydroxide  (NaOH)  was  shown  to  recover  the  flux almost completely. However, subsequent flux decline after cleaning was more rapid due to the presence of remaining crystals on the membrane surface, which acted as nucleation sites.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Chemical cleaning
texts.text: 4.1. Chemical cleaning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ideal chemical cleaning method would remove all foulants and scalants  from  the  membrane  surface  to  recover  100%  of  the  initial distillate  flux  and  to  prevent  pore  wetting  without  affecting  the  mechanical stability of the membrane. In practice, chemical cleaning only recovers a portion of the membrane ' s original performance and may be required every few months [70] or more frequently, at the expense of the membrane lifespan [71]. The formation of sediments on the surface of membranes and repeated chemical cleaning can lead not only to surface wetting but also to surface polymer degradation (Fig. 12). This is due not only  to  the  interaction  of  the  chemical  agents  used,  but  also  to  the property of formed deposits, which may even mechanically damage the membrane matrix [72].
texts.text: The ideal chemical cleaning method would remove all foulants and scalants  from  the  membrane  surface  to  recover  100%  of  the  initial distillate  flux  and  to  prevent  pore  wetting  without  affecting  the  mechanical stability of the membrane. In practice, chemical cleaning only recovers a portion of the membrane ' s original performance and may be required every few months [70] or more frequently, at the expense of the membrane lifespan [71]. The formation of sediments on the surface of membranes and repeated chemical cleaning can lead not only to surface wetting but also to surface polymer degradation (Fig. 12). This is due not only  to  the  interaction  of  the  chemical  agents  used,  but  also  to  the property of formed deposits, which may even mechanically damage the membrane matrix [72].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moreover,  residual  deposits  that  remain  on  the  membrane  after cleaning may lead to premature wetting either by creating hydrophilic bridges or by serving as nucleation sites for scale formation [72,73]. For this reason, the effects of cyclic washing of MD modules are different from those observed in the case of pressure driven processes (Fig. 13). In their case, the permeate quality and module performance are recovered after each washing cycle. If there is irreversible fouling, it is not possible to  completely  remove  impurities  and  the  module ' s  performance  systematically decreases. It is similar in the case of MD modules, but here the partial wetting of the membrane can result in the systematic deterioration in permeate quality, with less effective recovery of permeate quality with each cycle. This is often illustrated by the increase in the electrical conductivity of the obtained permeate. Eventually, irreversible fouling and operational damage to the membrane pores necessitates the replacement of the membrane module.

texts.text: 4.1.1. Selection of chemical agents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane  cleaning  is  an  essential  practice  in  membrane-based separation  processes  and  is  typically  conducted  in  a  routine  way  to ensure stable operation over time. The frequency with which cleaning processes are undertaken depends on a variety of factors including the type of separation process in question, feed water quality, process conditions, membrane design and others. Chemical treatments, such as with chlorine or acids can be very effective in restoring membrane performance but will result in contamination of the reject stream which may increase the costs of its treatment or disposal. Physical methods, such as aeration or backwashing don ' t face this issue but they may not be as effective  and will increase the energy consumption of the separation

texts.orig: Certain chemicals can target specific foulants. Alkaline solutions are particularly  effective  against  organic  foulants,  where  the  chemical bonds are broken down to make the foulant easier to remove. Strong alkaline chemicals like sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) are also effective for the removal of biofouling. However, frequent use can also corrode the membrane layer and lead to damage [71]. This occurs when the hypochlorite acid (HClO) and ion (ClO-) oxidize chemical groups in the polymer chain, leading to chain breaking and deformation of the pores [74]. Prolonged exposure leads to a reduction in the mechanical properties and physical structure of the membrane, which is described as ' ageing ' [71]. texts.orig: Table 1 summarises the major chemicals used to clean membranes in MD. The portion by which the distillate flux and solute rejection has been recovered to their original values often defines the efficacy of a cleaning regime in MD. The liquid entry pressure and contact angles may also be measured to monitor changes in the membrane ' s hydrophobicity. The number of cleaning cycles and their effect on membrane mechanical  strength  (usually  measured  by  Mullen  burst  pressure)  are important also for understanding the long-term impacts of the chemical cleaning method and assessing its stability [75]. Such tests need to be carried out on real-pilot scale systems to provide a more realistic picture.

texts.orig: The study by Guillen-Burrieza et al. [75] investigated the impacts of several  different  cleaning  agents,  such  as  DI  water,  EDTA/Na5P3O10, citric and oxalic acids, formic and sulphuric acid, on the contact angle and mechanical strength of a fouled membrane in synthetic seawater AGMD. The results found also that cleaning using only deionised water caused the lowest contact angle recovery (71% after 1 cleaning cycle) but had the smallest impact on the mechanical strength of the membrane ( + 2%). Overall, the best chemical cleaning reagent was a mixture of 0.1 wt% oxalic acid with 0.8 wt% citric acid, which recovered 98% of the membrane ' s original hydrophobicity while reducing the Mullen burst pressure by only 2%.

texts.orig: Long-term studies are particularly important when a scaling layer is formed on the surface of membranes. The cyclic removal of sediment from the membrane surface can lead to polymer degradation [18]. In another  work,  polypropylene  hollow  fibre  membranes  with  a  fluorosilicone coating were investigated for their biofouling propensity. It was shown that biofilm formation reduced flux values by depressing the vapour pressure and increasing temperature polarization. Nevertheless, the  initial  flux  values  were  restored  after  performing  a  cleaning sequence in which they flushed the membrane with NaOH (pH = 12 at 40 O C) followed by distilled water, ethanol (70%) to sterilise it and then sterilised distilled water to remove the ethanol [81].

The cyclic removal of sediment from the membrane surface can lead to polymer degradation [18]. In another  work,  polypropylene  hollow  fibre  membranes  with  a  fluorosilicone coating were investigated for their biofouling propensity. It was shown that biofilm formation reduced flux values by depressing the vapour pressure and increasing temperature polarization. Nevertheless, the  initial  flux  values  were  restored  after  performing  a  cleaning sequence in which they flushed the membrane with NaOH (pH = 12 at 40 O C) followed by distilled water, ethanol (70%) to sterilise it and then sterilised distilled water to remove the ethanol [81].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.2. Chemical cleaning regime
texts.text: 4.1.2. Chemical cleaning regime

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While the selection of chemical species is important to develop a good cleaning regime, the overall effectiveness depends on many other factors. Intermittent operation of MD, for example, can lead to the formation of crystals inside the pores, causing permanent damage from crystallisation fouling and pore wetting. In the aforementioned study by Guillen-Burrieza et al. [75] there was an investigation into the long-term impacts of membrane fouling in a pilot MD system. The study suggested that between operating periods, MD modules should be rinsed with pure water and stored in freshwater or completely dried to avoid re-wetting. This finding is in agreement with another pilot study [55]. Also, intermittent operation can cause temporary high permeate conductivity due to  leaking of the  feed solution through membrane defects [82]. This phenomenon is only temporary until the leaked feed water is diluted by more permeate production, but it signifies the importance of appropriate shut-down and start-up procedures in MD. This has been confirmed by experimental  studies  carried  out  with  different  commercial  modules [76]. Other chemical cleaning procedures recommended through the long-term pilot study include: thorough drying of the membranes postcleaning;  rinsing  the  membranes  with  large  amounts  of  water  to ensure sufficient removal of deposits; cleaning at low pressures to avoid compacting the fouling layer and cleaning at 40 ◦ C to increase transport, solubility of fouling material and faster cleaning reaction kinetics [75].

texts.orig: Furthermore,  surface  pore  wetting  is  unavoidable  when  scaling occurs. Initially, when pores are non-wetted, the CaCO3 scale deposits is formed mainly on the membrane surface (Fig. 14A). However, as a result of repeated washing of the module, the surface pores are wetted, which causes carbonate deposits to form also in the pores (Fig. 14B). Therefore, it is important in this case to limit the depth at which the salt crystals grow into the membrane pores. One reported solution was to increase the frequency of sediment removal, which for CaCO3  was achieved by dosing a portion of HCl at the inlet to the MD module every 1 -2 h [83]. In this process, the feed became a scrubbing solution, allowing the MD process to not be interrupted while the concentration of CaCl2  in the retentate increased slightly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: HCl solutions are very effective for the removal of CaCO3  deposits, however, a study on the cleaning effectiveness of different concentrations of HCl reported that repetitive cleaning cycles led to reduced MD performance due to silica scaling in CSG RO brine water recovery [41]. Silica scaling on the membrane surface contributes to flux and hydrophobicity decline but cannot be removed by acid reagents alone. This suggests  the  dual  cleaning  reagents  are  necessary  to  fully  recover membrane  performance.  However,  the  study  found  that  membrane scaling could be significantly alleviated by lowering the feed temperature, as this reduces precipitation. Adopting a low feed temperature of 35 ◦ C meant that high water recoveries of 80% were achievable without any observed scaling [41]. Lower feed temperatures could reduce the need  for  chemical  cleaning  but  it  comes  at  the  expense  of  lower permeate flux.

texts.orig: 4.2. Physical cleaning
texts.text: 4.2. Physical cleaning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to chemical cleaning, physical cleaning strategies can be employed in MD to mitigate performance decline. These can include air backwashing, aeration, vibration/ultrasound and water flushing although compared to chemical techniques, these have been less-widely explored in the literature (see Fig. 15).

texts.orig: 4.2.1. Backwashing
texts.text: 4.2.1. Backwashing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Backwashing (or backflushing) is a commonly used cleaning strategy employed for pressure-driven membrane processes such as ultrafiltration and is widely used in membrane bioreactors (MBRs). It involves flowing a fluid (either permeate or air) in the opposite direction to the permeate flow, thereby forcing any deposited foulants away from the surface of the pores, opening them up. Warsinger et al. [84] compared the LEP of pre-wetted PVDF MD membranes that underwent a > 48 hr dry-out compared to those which underwent air backwashing in-situ. In case of the feed solution 0.5 wt% NaCl, using pressurised air at 450 kPa for 10 s on the permeate side to remove the trapped water in the pores resulted  in  a  21%  decrease  in  the  LEP.  At  20  wt%  NaCl,  the  LEP reduction  was  greater  (45%).  However,  the  dried  out  membranes

texts.orig: The positive effects of using air backwashing were also reported by Julian et al. [85] who used a submerged vacuum membrane distillationcrystallisation  (VMDC)  system  to  treat  modelled  inland  desalination brine.  The  maximum  permeate  production  was  obtained  when  performing a 30 s air backwash at 200 kPa every 60 min. This delayed the onset of flux decline which nevertheless occurred after just over 20 h, compared to just 5 h when no backwashing was used. Increasing the duration and frequency of the backwashing procedure resulted in more magnesium  deposit  on  the  membrane  surface  and  inside  the  pores, creating a denser foulant layer which reduced the flux They also noted that  the  air  flow  increased  crystal  production  due  to  the  creation  of nucleation  sites,  suggesting  a  dual  purpose  for  air  backwashing  in membrane crystallisation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition, air-backwashing was found to help reduce CaSO4 scaling in certain conditions [86]. It was noted that higher feed temperatures resulted in reduced induction times for crystal formation, owing to the retrograde solubility of CaSO4, but noted that the rate of flux decline was very similar at each temperature. This suggests that the temperature affected the nucleation rate more than the growth rate. To control this, the authors flowed compressed air (100 kPa) back through the membrane pores for 3 min every 2 h. They noted that when the crystallisation process  was  heterogeneous,  meaning  it  occurred  on  the  membrane surface, there was a two-stage flux pattern in which the flux was stable initially and then rapidly declined at a critical concentration value. In this case, air backwashing had no effect on reducing flux decline. This was due to the inability of the air to remove the strongly adhered fouling layer.  However,  when  crystallisation  was  homogeneous,  meaning  it occurred  in  the  bulk  feed,  the  flux  decline  was  gradual  and  began immediately. In this case air backwashing was able to reduce the flux decline by 54% over 15 h compared to the membrane without treatment. Effective recovery of the flux was achieved by flushing the surface with DI water to remove the deposited crystals and achieve a concentration  factor  of  5.6  from  a  starting  concentration  of  1800  mg/L  of CaSO4. at 75 ◦ C using submerged vacuum membrane distillation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.2. Aeration
texts.text: 4.2.2. Aeration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aeration (bubbling or air-scouring) utilises the shear forces imparted by air bubbles onto the membrane surface to reduce the build-up of a fouling layer. In a static MD experiment which consisted of hydrophobic and superhydrophobic membranes placed on top of a beaker containing fouling  solutions,  Warsinger  et  al.  [87]  investigated  the  effects  of recharging the air-layer at the membrane-liquid interface and found that recharging  the  air  layer  helped  to  reduce  biofouling  by  up  to  96%. Nevertheless, dry-out of the membrane surface ran the risk of increased inorganic scaling. Separately, Ye et al. [88] found microbubbling aeration to improve significantly the performance of VMD when treating high salinity (100 mg/L) simulated seawater brine. Operation time prior to  scaling  was  extended  3-fold  with  the  use  of  microbubbling  at  a pressure of 400 KPa (lower pressure resulted in less of an increase). This enabled a proportional increase in water production and contributed less than 3% of the energy consumption of the process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.3. Vibration/ultrasound
texts.text: 4.2.3. Vibration/ultrasound

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use of vibration techniques such as ultrasound is another physical cleaning approach that works by using a transducer that produces high frequency vibrations when an electrical current is passed through it.  These vibrations cause cavitation, which is the formation of small vapour-filled voids or bubbles, in the surrounding liquid. When these cavities collapse or implode, they generate shockwaves which produce a physical  force  capable  of  dislodging  weakly  attached  particles  from surfaces.  Some  advantages  of  this  approach  include  chemical  free operation,  suitability  for  in-situ  treatment  and  potential  for  simultaneous cleaning and flux enhancement.

These vibrations cause cavitation, which is the formation of small vapour-filled voids or bubbles, in the surrounding liquid. When these cavities collapse or implode, they generate shockwaves which produce a physical  force  capable  of  dislodging  weakly  attached  particles  from surfaces.  Some  advantages  of  this  approach  include  chemical  free operation,  suitability  for  in-situ  treatment  and  potential  for  simultaneous cleaning and flux enhancement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Meng  et  al.  [89]  investigated  the  use  of  transverse  vibration  to reduce fouling in submerged VMD applications for inland desalination. The  flux  increased  with  frequency  up  to  a  tested  value  of  6.4  Hz, although the optimum in terms of energy efficiency was 4.8 Hz. This was attributed  to  a  reduction  in  temperature  polarisation.  Despite  this, fouling was not reduced significantly by the use of vibration. The authors also noted the detrimental effect of combining agitation with airbackwashing  and  aeration.  The  onset  of  severe  flux  decline  was observed at around 55 h of testing when using agitation alone compared to ~ 4 h for agitation + aeration and ~ 35 h for agitation + air-backwashing. This further supports the idea that air bubbles in the feed act as nucleation sites for crystal formation and can have counterproductive effects.

[91] who used in-line low power (8 -23 W) ultrasound to not only remove calcium and silica scaling on PTFE and PVDF membranes effectively, but also increase the flux by over 50 -70% due to improved mass transfer. They treated RO reject water and natural groundwater with AGMD. While higher power ultrasound further enhanced the flux, the authors noted that this can lead to degradation of the membrane and so lower-power use not only preserved the membrane but also reduced energy requirements.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.4. Water flushing
texts.text: 4.2.4. Water flushing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water flushing can be an effective strategy to manage scaling in MD, as mentioned earlier. Nghiem et al. [92] compared the effects of CaSO4 and CaCO3 scaling on MD performance and found the former to have a more severe effect on flux decline. By interrupting MD operation every 20 h and flushing the membrane surface with Milli-Q water, the authors were able to remove nucleation sites and hence prevent scale formation and preserve the flux. In this case, new feed water was introduced after each cleaning cycle and so water recovery didn ' t exceed 30%. However, it remained in a supersaturated condition. By flushing the membrane with water frequently enough it was possible to 'reset ' the induction time for crystal formation and prevent severe scaling. In other work, Jang et al. [93] compared the fouling propensities of reverse osmosis (RO), forward osmosis (FO) and MD using colloidal silica and alginate as model foulants. They found that FO had the greatest fouling propensity of  all,  due  to  the  effect  of  cake-enhanced concentration polarization, whereas MD had the lowest. However, they found that the efficacy of physical cleaning was greatest for FO and lowest for MD, suggesting less reversible fouling was taking place in MD. The flux recovery after this was highest for FO and RO and lowest for MD which was likely due to stronger interactions between the foulants and the hydrophobic surface of  the  MD  membrane  compared  with  the  hydrophilic  FO  and  RO membranes.

However, CaCO3 scale required rinsing with 5 wt% HCl solution to be removed from the surface of polypropylene membranes. In this work, high retentions of inorganic  and  organic  substances  (98  and  99%,  respectively)  were achieved in conjunction with membrane cleaning.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Self-cleaning membranes
texts.text: 4.3. Self-cleaning membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The lotus effect -the self-cleaning property of the leaves of the lotus flower -has inspired researchers to develop analogous superhydrophobic surfaces which impart self-cleaning properties on surfaces for various applications. With this perspective, superhydrophobic MD membranes have been fabricated to overcome fouling/wetting issues due to their much higher wetting resistance and self-cleaning properties [17,94].  Ma  et  al  [95]  developed  one  of  the  first  superhydrophobic membranes for MD applications. Further, superhydrophobic membranes demonstrated improved wetting resistance against feed solutions containing low surface tension components as reported by previous scholars [95-97]. However, superhydrophobic surfaces are recognized to still be prone to severe organic fouling. Self-cleaning properties of membranes against inorganic/organic scaling have been reported in a small number of articles [98-101]. For instance, Guo et al [101] developed an innovative electrospun photocatalytic self-cleaning BiOBr/Ag MD membrane for treating dyeing wastewater. In this study the usage of a BrOBr/Ag photocatalyst membrane had a dual benefit, one was to increase the hydrophobicity of the neat MD membrane, and the second was its ability to be regenerated under visible light. Separately, CNT-PVA was blended with  a  PP  polymer  to  form  an  electrically  conducting  composite  PP membrane which demonstrated improved cleaning ability against silica colloidal  scaling  [100].  Compared  with  superhydrophobic  surfaces, omniphobic surfaces have lower surface tension and so can repel liquids over  a  broad  range  of  surface  tensions  and  polarities,  including  low surface tension liquids such as oils. A range of methods for fabricating omniphobic membranes for MD applications been mentioned elsewhere [17]. It is of interest to explore the combination of omniphobic membranes with the cleaning strategies mentioned above to evaluate the overall efficacy of this approach to membrane development.

Further, superhydrophobic membranes demonstrated improved wetting resistance against feed solutions containing low surface tension components as reported by previous scholars [95-97]. However, superhydrophobic surfaces are recognized to still be prone to severe organic fouling. Self-cleaning properties of membranes against inorganic/organic scaling have been reported in a small number of articles [98-101]. For instance, Guo et al [101] developed an innovative electrospun photocatalytic self-cleaning BiOBr/Ag MD membrane for treating dyeing wastewater. In this study the usage of a BrOBr/Ag photocatalyst membrane had a dual benefit, one was to increase the hydrophobicity of the neat MD membrane, and the second was its ability to be regenerated under visible light. Separately, CNT-PVA was blended with  a  PP  polymer  to  form  an  electrically  conducting  composite  PP membrane which demonstrated improved cleaning ability against silica colloidal  scaling  [100].  Compared  with  superhydrophobic  surfaces, omniphobic surfaces have lower surface tension and so can repel liquids over  a  broad  range  of  surface  tensions  and  polarities,  including  low surface tension liquids such as oils. A range of methods for fabricating omniphobic membranes for MD applications been mentioned elsewhere [17]. It is of interest to explore the combination of omniphobic membranes with the cleaning strategies mentioned above to evaluate the overall efficacy of this approach to membrane development.

texts.orig: 5. Conclusions and future outlook
texts.text: 5. Conclusions and future outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, recent advances in membrane cleaning and pretreatment in MD for water reclamation have been reviewed and their summary  can  be  found  in  Table  2.  While  membrane  fouling  cannot  be avoided completely in MD, various cleaning and pretreatment protocols can be employed to manage and mitigate it. For managing inorganic fouling, dilute acid cleaning solutions such as citric or acetic acid are highly effective at recovering flux and permeate conductivity values, although residual crystals on membrane surfaces may act as nucleation sites for subsequent and more rapid crystal formation once operation begins again. In addition, the long-term use of these acids may result in degradation of the membrane -something that needs further investigation. The use of antiscalants is a more cost-effective way to prevent scaling  in  the  first  place  (at  least  for  moderate  recovery  ratios). Furthermore,  the  growing  interest  in  biodegradable  antiscalants  is demonstrating  the capabilities of less  environmentally  damaging chemicals  in  these  applications.  In  addition,  simple  approaches  like mesh pre-filtration, can provide a 'sacrificial ' substrate on which crystals can  favourably  grow,  protecting  the  membrane  itself  from  scaling. Simple removal of the salt by periodically cleaning the mesh could be a cost-effective measure to control scaling, particularly for high recovery applications and certainly warrants more investigation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Organic fouling is a particular challenge in MD due to the attractive hydrophobic  interactions  between  the  membrane  and  organic  molecules.  Alkaline  solutions  such  as  NaOH  have  proven  effective  at removing organic substances from the membrane and restoring performance. Although, irreversible fouling (which seems to affect MD more so  than  RO  and  FO)  may  limit  the  long-term  effectiveness  of  this cleaning method. Results that are more promising come from preventing organic  fouling  in  the  first  place  by  use  of  pretreatment,  such  as coagulation-flocculation, foam  fractionation, adsorption,  advanced oxidation processes and pre-filtration. Given that these methods have been proven to be cost-effective in pressure-driven membrane separation processes already, it is recommended to investigate them in combination with MD as there are currently a relatively limited number of such studies in the literature. It seems that even a single pretreatment step is sufficient to maintain stable MD operation, whereas pressuredriven  applications  tend  to  require  several.  This  suggests  that  while MD is still prone to fouling, it may in fact be easier, cheaper and more environmentally  friendly  to  manage  than  in  pressure-driven  applications. Controlling organic and biofouling in MD will increase its suitability  for  applications  like  produced  water  treatment,  wastewater treatment, food & beverage production, agricultural water treatment and others where organic loads may be high. As such, it is of great interest to develop cost-effective strategies for such fouling-mitigation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Physical cleaning methods include: air backwashing; aeration; water flushing  and  ultrasound/vibration.  The  comparative  lack  of  studies conducted in these areas mean there are some seemingly contradictory conclusions in the literature. Methods such as aeration have been shown to enhance performance stability in some cases and hinder it in others. It seems clear, however, that while air bubbles may physically hinder scale formation,  they  may  also  initiate  the  nucleation  of  crystals  in  high salinity feed waters either in the bulk liquid or on the membrane surface.

texts.orig: This may be detrimental but it can also be advantageous in applications such  as  membrane  crystallisation,  where  the  formation  of  crystals  is intentional. Aeration also seems effective at reducing biofouling in MD, although  more  research  is  needed  in  this  area  to  ascertain  the  best strategies for managing this issue. Meanwhile, cleaning the membrane with Milli-Q water can effectively remove soluble scale deposits and restore performance but again, any residual crystals on the surface or in the  pores  can  result  in  more  rapid  scaling  as  they  serve  as  crystal nucleation sites. Ultrasound and vibration have been shown to remove calcium and silica scaling effectively but care must be taken not to use high power or high frequencies that may damage the membrane over time. Low power ultrasound and low frequency vibration seem promising  approaches  for  further  investigation  and  may  not  significantly affect the energy consumption of the process.

table_cells.text: Purpose, most common examples and target foulants

Removes foulants from the membrane surface through surfactant interactions with hydrophobic foulants or chemical interactions with scalants. • NaOCl (organic and biological foulants)[1] • EDTA and NaOH (organic foulants, i.e. humic acid)[2]

• Mechanical damage leading to weaker membranes. • Wetting of the membrane surface. • Non-biodegradable. • Can produce harmful by-products. • Generates chemical-containing waste which needs to be safely disposed of.
Physically dislodges foulants from the membrane surface. • Backwash (e.g. CaSO4)[5] • and scaling [6,7] • Flushing (e.g. CaCO3)[8]

• Not effective for removal of irreversible fouling. • Examination of the impact of repetitive pressurization on membrane performance is required under realistic operating conditions. • Specific spacer and membrane design is required to
• Increased pumping requirements and cost. • Wetting of the membrane surface. • Not effective for removal of irreversible fouling.
Creates cavities that generate shockwaves to physically dislodge foulants from the membrane surface. • Vibration/ultrasound (e.g. Silica scaling)[13,14]

• Membrane damage. • Ultra-sound assisted membrane processes are currently impractical. • Additional equipment costs. • The effectiveness of ultrasound in flux enhancement and cleaning of different types of membrane modules

Removes foulants or inactivates scalants from the feed solution, prior to the MD process • Adsorption (e.g. acetic acid)[16] • Coagulation/flocculation (e.g. CaCO3)[17] • Textile WW)[18,19] • AOPs (e.g. VOCs, parraffines, azo dyes)[20 - 22]

• Generates waste sludge which needs to be disposed of. • Additional separation stage may be necessary to provide a good solid - liquid separation, for example, filtration, sedimentation, or centrifugation. • High cost of the adsorbents.
• Additional cost from coagulant chemicals. • Coagulants to freshwater/marine environments. • Weak selectivity against some proteins and anionic surfactants.
• Additional electricity consumption. • Generates by-products which need to be safely disposed of. • Further research required.
• Additional membrane cost. • Environmentally-friendly antiscalants required.
• Antiscalants containing phosphorous can accelerate the growth of microbes.
